channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: UK house prices expected to keep falling as mortgage rates climb
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices are anticipated to face downward pressure in the coming months, driven by rising mortgage rates and ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. Experts suggest that these factors, combined with weakened consumer confidence and broader economic uncertainty, could further dampen buyer activity and affordability across the property market.

Mortgage Rates and Market Sentiment

Recent global developments, including heightened geopolitical tensions, have led to increased volatility in financial markets. This has prompted lenders to reassess interest rate expectations, resulting in higher borrowing costs for prospective homeowners. Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, highlighted that these rising mortgage rates are likely to place gradual downward pressure on house prices. Whilst some buyers may rush to complete purchases before favourable mortgage offers expire, others could find their spending power significantly reduced.

Bill also noted that the duration of the current geopolitical disruption and its impact on energy prices will play a crucial role in shaping the housing market’s trajectory. Should instability persist or energy costs rise further, this could continue to weigh on buyer sentiment, particularly amongst those already cautious due to the ongoing cost-of-living crisis.

Recent House Price Trends

The latest data from Halifax’s house price index reveals that UK property prices have fallen for two consecutive months. In April, the average home price dropped by 0.1% to £299,313, following a 0.5% decline in March. Annual house price growth has also slowed to 0.4%, down from 0.8% previously. Amanda Bryden, head of mortgages at Halifax, attributed this softening to increased global uncertainty, which has influenced inflation expectations and pushed borrowing costs higher.

Bryden emphasised that these factors are likely to make households more cautious, particularly as the cost of living remains a pressing concern. This shift in sentiment could lead to reduced demand in the housing market, further contributing to price declines in the short term.

Outlook for the Year Ahead

Despite the current downward trend, Knight Frank anticipates that modest growth in house prices could return later in the year. However, this recovery will largely depend on the resolution of geopolitical tensions, the government’s fiscal response to economic challenges, and wider market stability. Bill suggested that whilst short-term pressures are inevitable, the housing market’s resilience could see it regain momentum by the end of 2024.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, navigating this period of uncertainty may require careful planning. Staying informed about market trends and seeking independent financial advice could help individuals make informed decisions tailored to their circumstances.

As the UK housing market adapts to evolving economic and geopolitical factors, stakeholders will be closely monitoring developments to gauge the potential impact on prices, affordability, and buyer confidence in the months ahead.

item.title: New property laws set to feature in the King’s Speech
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The King’s Speech, set to take place this Wednesday, will outline the government’s legislative agenda for the upcoming Parliamentary session. Among the key policy areas expected to feature, housing reforms are anticipated to take centre stage, with potential implications for homeowners, leaseholders, and the wider property sector.

Leasehold and Commonhold Reform Bill

One of the housing-related bills expected to be highlighted is the Leasehold and Commonhold Reform Bill. This legislation, which is already in progress, aims to expand leaseholders’ rights in England and Wales. Key proposals include making it easier for leaseholders to extend their leases and purchase freeholds, which could provide greater financial security and control over their properties.

Additionally, the bill seeks to cap ground rents at £250 per year, with plans to reduce these to a nominal “peppercorn” rent after 40 years. These changes follow earlier draft legislation published this year and could significantly reduce the financial burden on leaseholders, many of whom have faced escalating ground rent costs in recent years.

Building Safety Remediation Bill

Another anticipated bill is the Building Safety Remediation Bill, which focuses on strengthening building safety standards across the UK. This legislation builds on the government’s existing remediation policy framework, introduced in response to the tragic Grenfell Tower fire in 2017. The bill aims to implement further measures to ensure that buildings meet stringent safety requirements, providing greater protection for residents and addressing ongoing concerns about fire safety in high-rise developments.

The proposals could also include provisions to hold developers and building owners accountable for safety defects, ensuring that necessary remediation work is carried out promptly and effectively. This bill is likely to be closely monitored by both the construction industry and homeowners, particularly those living in high-risk properties.

What This Means for the Property Sector

The inclusion of these bills in the King’s Speech signals the government’s continued focus on housing and property-related issues. For leaseholders, the proposed reforms could mark a significant step towards fairer and more transparent agreements, whilst the building safety measures aim to restore confidence in the safety of residential properties.

However, the final details of these bills will only be confirmed on the day of the speech, leaving some uncertainties for the sector. Property professionals and homeowners alike may wish to stay informed as further announcements are made, particularly regarding how these reforms could impact their financial and legal obligations.

As the government seeks to reset its agenda following recent political challenges, housing policy remains a priority. The King’s Speech will provide a clearer picture of the legislative direction for the coming year, with potential long-term implications for the UK property market.

item.title: UK rental market shows growing regional divergence in latest figures
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK rental market is displaying significant regional variations, with some areas experiencing sharp rises in rents whilst others are seeing declines, according to recent data from Propertymark. This divergence highlights the contrasting pressures faced by tenants and landlords across different parts of the country.

Strong Rental Growth in Scotland and Northern Ireland

Scotland has emerged as one of the regions with the most significant monthly rental increases, with average rents climbing from £1,123 to £1,167 – a 3.9% rise. This surge underscores the ongoing demand for rental properties in the region, coupled with a tight supply of available homes. Similarly, Northern Ireland also witnessed notable growth, with average rents increasing by 3.7% from £887 to £920 per month.

Interestingly, despite these rises, the average salary required to meet affordability thresholds in Northern Ireland has decreased by 2.4% year-on-year to £27,600. This suggests that while rents are climbing, affordability pressures may be easing slightly for some tenants.

London's Rental Market Rebounds

London’s rental market has shown signs of recovery after a recent slowdown, with average monthly rents rising by 3% from £2,193 to £2,259. This rebound reflects the capital’s enduring appeal to renters, despite its traditionally higher costs. However, affordability remains a key concern, with tenants needing an annual salary of £67,770 to secure an average-priced home in the city.

Declining Rents in Wales and Northern England

In contrast to the growth seen in Scotland, Northern Ireland, and London, several regions have recorded falling rents, signalling a potential market correction. Wales experienced a 3.4% decline in average rents, whilst the North East and North West saw monthly drops of 3% and 2.6% respectively. These decreases may offer some relief to tenants in these areas, where affordability has been a persistent issue.

The data provides a snapshot of how rental costs are evolving relative to income levels across the UK. With such stark regional differences, tenants and landlords alike may benefit from keeping a close eye on local market trends to make informed decisions.

item.title: Estate agents accused of pricing above market expectations
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

New research suggests estate agents in most UK regions are setting asking prices above what buyers are currently prepared to pay, creating growing disparities between initial listings and final sale prices. This pricing mismatch comes during a period of market adjustment, where higher mortgage costs and affordability pressures are reshaping buyer behaviour across the country.

Regional disparities in pricing strategies

Analysis from House Buyer Bureau reveals only London and the South East currently show relative alignment between seller expectations and market values. In these regions, asking prices sit slightly below estimated market values, with gaps of -£113,197 and -£6,977 respectively.

Elsewhere, the picture differs markedly:

  • South West England shows the largest gap at +£45,086 above market value
  • Yorkshire and the Humber follows at +£33,459
  • North West England records +£26,632
  • East Midlands displays +£26,262 above estimated values

Market friction and selling times

These pricing disparities appear to be contributing to longer selling periods and increased price reductions in some areas. Chris Hodgkinson of House Buyer Bureau notes London and South East sellers have been particularly proactive in adjusting expectations downward, often beyond what current conditions might require.

Meanwhile, other regions show sellers maintaining more optimistic pricing strategies despite changing market fundamentals. This divergence in approaches across different parts of the country may be creating uneven market conditions, with some areas experiencing smoother transactions than others.

As the property market continues to adapt to new financial realities, homeowners considering selling might wish to research local pricing trends carefully. Estate agents' valuations could potentially benefit from closer alignment with current buyer affordability levels and recent comparable sales in their specific area.

item.title: Modern conveyancing practices facing scrutiny as property transaction delays worsen
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Delays in property transactions across England and Wales are sparking concerns about modern conveyancing practices, with experts warning that structural pressures within the system need urgent attention. The Conveyancing Task Force (CTF) has highlighted a range of issues contributing to slower processes, from lender hold-ups to complications with leasehold properties. Whilst recent discussions have focused on digitisation and administrative reforms, the CTF argues these measures may not address deeper operational challenges.

What’s causing transaction delays?

According to the CTF, property transactions are being hindered by multiple factors. Mortgage-related issues, survey renegotiations, and fragile property chains are frequently cited as causes of hold-ups. Additionally, leasehold complications and increasing legal complexity are adding to the time it takes to complete a sale. The group also pointed to operational practices within some high-volume conveyancing firms, where large caseloads and limited supervision can lead to poor communication and accountability.

Stephen Larcombe, speaking on behalf of the CTF, emphasised the frustration felt by consumers. Many homeowners are spending significant sums of money, chasing updates for weeks, and struggling to maintain continuity with their conveyancer. When transactions collapse, the resulting stress can be immense. Larcombe stressed that conveyancing is a professional legal service requiring judgment and expertise, not merely an administrative task.

Are current reforms enough?

Parliament’s Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee has called for reforms to speed up transactions, including greater upfront information and earlier commitments from buyers and sellers. However, the CTF warns that focusing solely on administrative changes and digitisation risks overlooking deeper structural issues. The group argues that reforms must address professional standards, supervision, and accountability across the sector.

The CTF has also raised concerns about referral-driven business models, which can sometimes influence consumer choice and undermine independence within the transaction process. Policymakers have been urged to ensure that reform proposals are informed by both industry professionals and consumers navigating the system, rather than being dominated by larger institutional or commercial interests.

As the debate continues, it remains clear that improving the conveyancing process will require a balanced approach. Whilst technological advancements and administrative efficiency are important, addressing operational weaknesses and maintaining professional standards will be key to delivering a smoother experience for homeowners.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #435
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

As the Renters’ Reform Act comes into full effect, new challenges and trends are emerging in the private rental sector. From compliance concerns to shifting tenant attitudes, landlords and letting agents face a transformed landscape requiring careful navigation.

Low uptake of mandatory tenant information sheets raises concerns

Recent Freedom of Information data reveals surprisingly low download figures for the new tenant information sheet, a compulsory document under the updated legislation. With just 153,000 downloads recorded since publication – despite the requirement applying to all tenancies by 31st May – industry experts express concern about landlord engagement.

Whilst some landlords may be obtaining the form through letting agents or reusing downloaded copies, the figures appear disproportionately low for England’s private rental market. Failure to provide tenants with this document could result in substantial penalties of up to £7,000 per tenancy. Property owners might wish to verify their compliance procedures to avoid potential fines.

Tenancy management requires new approaches under reform act

The shift to periodic tenancies as standard under the Renters’ Reform Act brings fresh administrative challenges. Tim Frome of Mydeposits highlights how the removal of fixed-term agreements changes the rhythm of tenancy management, particularly regarding tenant changes during existing agreements.

Key considerations now include:

  • Whether to create new inventories for replacement tenants or adapt existing documents
  • Clear documentation of responsibility transfers between outgoing and incoming tenants
  • Particular care with HMO properties where multiple tenant changes may occur

Without robust processes, landlords and agents could face increased disputes over deposits and property conditions. Professional advice might prove valuable when establishing new tenancy management systems.

Changing attitudes see renting popularity grow

New research suggests a significant shift in tenant perceptions, with 41% reporting a more positive outlook on renting than ever before. The RentGuarantor survey reveals particular enthusiasm among 18-29 year olds, with many appreciating the flexibility renting offers.

Key findings include:

  • 30% of respondents actively choose renting for lifestyle reasons
  • 88% believe renting has improved compared to previous years
  • 56% value the ability to live in locations otherwise unaffordable to buy
  • 54% appreciate not being responsible for maintenance

Interestingly, nearly a third of over-60s also expressed preference for renting, suggesting the sector’s appeal spans generations. As housing affordability challenges persist, these shifting attitudes may reshape the rental market’s future.

With legislative changes and evolving tenant expectations, the private rental sector continues to transform. Both landlords and renters may benefit from staying informed about these developments as the market adapts to its new normal.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #432
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Councils across England are set to receive £41 million in government funding to prepare for the implementation of the Renters Reform Act, which comes into force on 1st May. This additional support follows an earlier allocation of £18.2 million, bringing the total investment to help local authorities manage their new enforcement responsibilities under the legislation.

Funding for Rental Sector Enforcement

The Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities has distributed the funding to 317 local councils in England. The money is intended to bolster enforcement capabilities as the Renters Reform Act introduces significant changes to the private rental sector. Local authorities may use these resources to tackle issues such as poor property conditions and unfair eviction practices.

This financial support could prove crucial for councils needing to expand their housing teams or develop new systems to handle tenant complaints and landlord compliance. The government has emphasised that the funding aims to create a fairer rental market whilst maintaining standards across the sector.

Preparing for the Renters Reform Act

With the Renters Reform Act’s implementation date approaching, landlords and letting agents might wish to familiarise themselves with the forthcoming changes. Key provisions include:

  • The abolition of Section 21 ‘no-fault’ evictions
  • New grounds for possession under Section 8
  • Strengthened protections against retaliatory evictions
  • Introduction of a Private Rented Sector Ombudsman

Property owners may need to review their tenancy agreements and procedures to ensure compliance with the new regulations. Meanwhile, tenants could benefit from understanding their enhanced rights under the legislation.

As the rental market adapts to these reforms, both landlords and local authorities face a period of adjustment. The additional funding represents a significant step in supporting councils to enforce the new rules effectively, potentially leading to improved standards in the private rented sector.

item.title: Estate agents are facing a new trust challenge
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Estate agents are navigating a growing challenge around trust and transparency as consumer expectations regarding artificial intelligence (AI) continue to shift. Recent research from iamproperty highlights an emerging "AI trust gap," with 65% of consumers stating that agents should clearly disclose when AI is used in communications or property listings. Meanwhile, around a quarter of respondents expressed discomfort with the technology altogether. These findings, detailed in The Real eSTATE of It report, underscore the delicate balance between AI adoption and maintaining client confidence.

The AI Trust Gap in Estate Agency

The report reveals that AI is already deeply integrated into estate agency workflows, with over 60% of agents using it to streamline administrative tasks, enhance staff capacity, and automate repetitive processes. However, whilst agents are embracing AI to improve efficiency, consumer expectations around transparency appear to be lagging behind. This disconnect has the potential to influence how estate agency services are perceived, particularly in an industry where trust has long been a cornerstone of client relationships.

Jamie Cooke, co-founder of iamproperty, emphasises the importance of addressing this gap. "The conversation around AI has largely focused on its capabilities and how agents are utilising it," he says. "However, our data suggests that the real issue lies in the erosion of trust that agents have worked hard to build. Consumers are seeking clarity—they want to know when AI is being used and how it enhances the service they receive."

Transparency and Training Are Key

To bridge the AI trust gap, Cooke suggests that agents must go beyond merely understanding the technology. Instead, they need to confidently explain its value to clients. "Transparency, training, and clear standards will be crucial in ensuring that AI strengthens trust rather than undermining it," he notes. This approach introduces a new layer of professional responsibility, requiring agents to establish internal standards, provide team training, and maintain consistent communication with clients throughout the transaction process.

The report also highlights that nearly half of consumers are comfortable with agents using AI to support transactions, provided there is transparency. "Technology should support agents, not replace them," Cooke adds. "The agents who will stand out are those who use AI to deliver an even better service, with clear and honest communication at the core. Getting this balance right can strengthen trust, especially in a more complex market."

As AI continues to reshape the estate agency landscape, maintaining trust through transparency and effective communication will be essential. For agents, adopting clear standards and prioritising client understanding could prove pivotal in navigating this evolving challenge successfully.

The AI Trust Gap in Estate Agency

The report reveals that AI is already deeply integrated into estate agency workflows, with over 60% of agents using it to streamline administrative tasks, enhance staff capacity, and automate repetitive processes. However, whilst agents are embracing AI to improve efficiency, consumer expectations around transparency appear to be lagging behind. This disconnect has the potential to influence how estate agency services are perceived, particularly in an industry where trust has long been a cornerstone of client relationships.

Jamie Cooke, co-founder of iamproperty, emphasises the importance of addressing this gap. "The conversation around AI has largely focused on its capabilities and how agents are utilising it," he says. "However, our data suggests that the real issue lies in the erosion of trust that agents have worked hard to build. Consumers are seeking clarity—they want to know when AI is being used and how it enhances the service they receive."

Transparency and Training Are Key

To bridge the AI trust gap, Cooke suggests that agents must go beyond merely understanding the technology. Instead, they need to confidently explain its value to clients. "Transparency, training, and clear standards will be crucial in ensuring that AI strengthens trust rather than undermining it," he notes. This approach introduces a new layer of professional responsibility, requiring agents to establish internal standards, provide team training, and maintain consistent communication with clients throughout the transaction process.

The report also highlights that nearly half of consumers are comfortable with agents using AI to support transactions, provided there is transparency. "Technology should support agents, not replace them," Cooke adds. "The agents who will stand out are those who use AI to deliver an even better service, with clear and honest communication at the core. Getting this balance right can strengthen trust, especially in a more complex market."

As AI continues to reshape the estate agency landscape, maintaining trust through transparency and effective communication will be essential. For agents, adopting clear standards and prioritising client understanding could prove pivotal in navigating this evolving challenge successfully.

item.title: What is currently happening in the UK property market?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The UK property market has shown mixed trends in recent weeks, with some indicators pointing to resilience whilst others suggest ongoing challenges for buyers and sellers. As we analyse the latest data up to early May 2026, several key patterns emerge that could help homeowners and prospective buyers understand current market conditions.

Sales and Listings Show Year-on-Year Variations

Year-to-date figures reveal 422,000 UK homes were sold subject to contract in 2026 – marking the strongest week for residential sales in 45 weeks. However, this remains 5.3% lower than the same period in 2025. When compared to pre-pandemic levels, the market appears more robust, with sales 12.5% higher than the 2017–2019 average.

Net residential sales, which account for fall-throughs, stand at 330,000 properties year-to-date. This represents a 3.1% decline from 2025 but remains comfortably above longer-term averages. Meanwhile, new listings have increased, with 637,000 properties coming to market – 1% higher than 2025 and 17.5% above the pre-Covid norm.

Regional Spotlight: Swansea Market Dynamics

In Swansea, estate and letting agents are employing various strategies to navigate the current market. Performance data suggests that agents who provide accurate valuations and avoid overpricing tactics tend to achieve better results. Approximately 46.7% of properties withdrawn from agents' books in April failed to sell, with overvaluation being a primary factor – particularly when combined with lengthy sole agency agreements exceeding 20 weeks.

For homeowners considering selling, these figures highlight the importance of realistic pricing from the outset. Properties priced correctly from launch typically achieve better outcomes than those requiring subsequent reductions.

Price Adjustments and Market Realities

The data shows 26,300 price reductions occurred in the latest recorded week, with 13.1% of UK residential properties for sale having undergone price adjustments in April. This remains slightly above the 2025 average of 12.8% and significantly higher than the six-year long-term average of 10.7%.

A notable trend is the widening gap between initial asking prices and eventual sale prices, currently standing at 27.4% compared to the 10-year average of 16–17%. This discrepancy suggests that whilst buyer demand exists, many are negotiating harder or waiting for better-value opportunities.

As the market continues to evolve, both buyers and sellers may benefit from careful research and professional advice to navigate these complex conditions effectively.

item.title: Rents fall again as rental market slowdown gathers pace
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New data reveals a continued slowdown in England's rental market, with average monthly rents dipping for the second consecutive month. According to the latest Goodlord Rental Index, rents fell by 0.6% nationally in April, bringing the average cost of a new tenancy to £1,205 per calendar month (pcm). This follows a period of sustained growth, with annual rental inflation now standing at just 1.7% – the lowest rate recorded since mid-2025.

Regional variations in rental performance

The April figures show significant regional disparities in rental market performance. The North East experienced the sharpest monthly decline at 4.9%, followed by 2.8% drops in both the West Midlands and Yorkshire and the Humber. Meanwhile, London bucked the trend with a 1.3% increase, and the East Midlands saw the strongest growth at 2.7%.

Annual comparisons reveal an even more complex picture. While London maintained the highest year-on-year growth at 4.8%, four regions actually recorded falling rents compared to April 2025. The North East showed the most dramatic reversal, moving from nearly 6% annual growth in March to a 0.9% decline by April.

Changing market dynamics

The rental slowdown coincides with several important market shifts. Void periods lengthened nationally from 22 to 24 days, with Yorkshire and the Humber seeing properties remain empty for an average of 29 days. This contrasts sharply with London's 17-day average void period, suggesting continued strong demand in the capital.

Industry analysts note that rental growth now trails both wage inflation (3.8%) and general inflation (3.4%), potentially improving affordability for some tenants. The data also provides a final snapshot before the implementation of the Renters' Rights Act on 1st May, which introduced significant reforms to the private rental sector.

As the market continues to adjust, both landlords and tenants may wish to monitor these evolving trends carefully. While some regions show signs of cooling, others demonstrate resilience, making local market knowledge increasingly important for rental decisions.

item.title: Giles Peaker: A Tribute to a Housing Law Pioneer
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The legal and housing communities are mourning the loss of Giles Peaker, a pioneering solicitor whose contributions to housing law have left a lasting impact. As a partner at London-based firm Anthony Gold, Peaker was widely recognised for his expertise in landlord and tenant law. His legacy includes not only his impressive legal career but also his efforts to improve housing standards across the UK.

The Nearly Legal Blog

One of Peaker’s most notable achievements was the creation of the Nearly Legal blog in 2006. Launched shortly after he began his legal journey, the blog quickly became a go-to resource for housing law professionals, landlords, and tenants alike. Its name, a nod to Peaker’s status as an unqualified solicitor at the time, reflected his ambition and dedication to the field. Over the years, Nearly Legal evolved into an invaluable tool, often cited in litigation and praised for its clarity and depth.

The blog’s success highlights Peaker’s commitment to making complex legal issues accessible. Many hope that the platform will continue to thrive, ensuring his work remains a resource for future generations.

A Lasting Legislative Impact

Beyond his blogging, Peaker played a crucial role in shaping housing policy. Alongside Karen Buck MP and Justin Bates KC, he was instrumental in the creation of the Homes (Fitness for Human Habitation) Act 2018. This private member’s bill, spearheaded by Buck, aimed to improve living conditions for tenants across England and Wales.

The Act, which Peaker helped draft and advocate for, ensures that rented properties must meet basic standards of habitability. Its introduction marked a significant step forward in tenant rights and has since benefited countless individuals living in substandard accommodation.

Recognition and Legacy

Peaker’s contributions to housing law were widely acknowledged throughout his career. In 2018, he was awarded the Legal Aid Lawyer of the Year Award for housing, a testament to his dedication to advocating for those in need. His work extended beyond the courtroom, influencing both policy and public understanding of housing issues.

Those who knew Peaker remember him not only for his professional achievements but also for his kindness and willingness to share his knowledge. His passing is a significant loss to the legal community, but his legacy continues through the laws he helped shape and the resources he created.

As the housing sector reflects on Peaker’s contributions, his work serves as a reminder of the importance of accessible legal resources and the impact of thoughtful legislation. His dedication to improving housing standards will undoubtedly continue to benefit tenants and professionals for years to come.

item.title: Drafting a new Renters’ Rights Act Tenancy Agreement – Series Overview
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act 2025, set to come into effect on 1 May, marks one of the most significant changes in landlord and tenant law in recent years. With the abolition of fixed-term tenancies and section 21 evictions, alongside new requirements for written terms and disclosures, landlords and letting agents may need to review and update their tenancy agreements to ensure compliance. A well-drafted agreement is essential to avoid potential penalties and ensure clarity for all parties involved.

Why the Renters’ Rights Act Matters

The Renters’ Rights Act introduces sweeping reforms designed to enhance tenant protections and improve transparency in the rental market. Key changes include the removal of fixed-term tenancies, meaning all rental agreements will become periodic tenancies, and the elimination of section 21 "no-fault" evictions. Additionally, landlords must now provide tenants with clear written terms and key information at the start of the tenancy.

For landlords, these changes mean ensuring tenancy agreements are legally compliant, fair, and robust enough to withstand potential challenges. Poorly drafted agreements could lead to difficulties in enforcement or even financial penalties. As such, it may be worth seeking professional guidance or using updated templates to align with the new legislation.

Key Considerations for Drafting Compliant Agreements

Creating a Renters’ Rights Act-compliant tenancy agreement involves several important steps. Firstly, landlords and agents must remove any clauses that are no longer permitted, such as those related to fixed terms or section 21 evictions. Secondly, the agreement should include a "Key Information" section, providing tenants with essential details about the tenancy in a clear and accessible format.

Other considerations include addressing matters such as utility bills, deposits, and compliance with the Equality Act 2010. Robust wording is crucial to ensure the agreement is enforceable and fair to both parties. Some landlords and agents may also explore using digital tools or AI-assisted platforms to streamline the drafting process, though it’s important to thoroughly review any automated outputs for accuracy and compliance.

As the Renters’ Rights Act 2025 approaches, landlords and letting agents may wish to take proactive steps to ensure their tenancy agreements meet the new legal requirements. By focusing on clarity, fairness, and compliance, they can help create a smoother rental experience for both themselves and their tenants.

item.title: House price trends underline more subdued market conditions
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent analysis of UK housing market trends reveals a mixed picture, with Scotland experiencing modest price growth whilst England and Wales saw declines over the past year. According to data from DJ Alexander, average house prices in Scotland increased by £3,532 between March 2025 and February 2026, representing a 1.9% annual rise. In contrast, England and Wales recorded a 0.6% drop, with average prices falling by £1,742 during the same period.

Regional Variations Across Scotland

The Scottish housing market displayed significant regional disparities. Inverclyde led the way with an impressive 11.3% annual increase, equating to a rise of £11,422 in average house prices. Other areas with notable growth included East Dunbartonshire (£10,880), Moray (£10,303), Renfrewshire (£10,138), and South Ayrshire (£9,727). However, not all regions fared well – Aberdeen experienced the steepest decline, with average prices falling by £7,517. Other areas such as East Lothian, Highland, Stirling, and Midlothian also saw price drops.

Edinburgh retained its position as Scotland’s most expensive housing market, with an average property price of £294,990. Despite this, the overall trend suggests a cooling market, with peak prices recorded in November 2025 (£193,044) followed by a 3.3% decline by February 2026.

Factors Influencing the Market

David Alexander, CEO of DJ Alexander Scotland, highlighted several factors contributing to the subdued market conditions. He noted that slower-than-expected interest rate reductions, the ongoing cost-of-living crisis, and rising unemployment have all played a role in dampening demand. Additionally, external economic pressures, such as anticipated fuel price increases following geopolitical events, are likely to further soften the market in the coming months.

Alexander also observed a shift in buyer behaviour, with some purchasers opting for more affordable locations outside city centres. This trend is reflected in the strong performance of areas surrounding Glasgow and rural regions like Moray and the Borders. The rise in remote working appears to be a key driver, enabling homeowners to prioritise affordability and lifestyle over proximity to urban hubs.

Whilst Scotland’s housing market has shown resilience compared to other parts of the UK, the data suggests a broader slowdown. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor these trends closely, particularly as economic uncertainties continue to shape the property landscape.

item.title: Rental supply squeeze hits four-year high
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rental market squeeze reaches four-year peak as landlords adapt to reforms

The UK rental market is facing its tightest supply conditions in four years, with landlords adjusting strategies ahead of new legislation and tenants facing dwindling options. The Renters’ Rights Act, which came into force on 1 May, has prompted some property owners to sell up or reclaim possession before the reforms took effect, whilst others have increased rents to offset rising compliance costs.

London rental values show strongest growth in nearly two years

Market data reveals prime outer London rents rose by 3% annually to April – the sharpest increase since June 2024. Prime central London saw a more modest 1.1% uplift over the same period. Industry experts note that rental growth is being fuelled by shrinking availability, with lettings listings in both prime central and outer London 15% below the five-year average during Q1 2026.

Mel Constantinou of Knight Frank observes: “The legislative changes are accelerating existing trends, with landlords refining pricing approaches and some exiting the sector altogether. Meanwhile, tenants are staying put longer as their options contract.” Current figures show nearly six prospective tenants competing for each new rental listing – the highest ratio since autumn 2022.

Regulatory changes compound existing pressures

The Renters’ Rights Act represents the latest challenge for landlords following recent tax changes including higher stamp duty and reduced reliefs. These measures have contributed to a 30% rise in prime central London rents over the past decade, with outer London seeing a 25% increase.

In higher-value markets, supply constraints have been less pronounced as more discretionary owners opt to let properties rather than sell during the current housing market slowdown. However, the overall shortage of available rentals continues to drive competition among tenants across all price brackets.

With the legislative landscape continuing to evolve, both landlords and tenants may wish to stay informed about how these changes could affect their housing decisions in the coming months.

item.title: Six ways the Renters Rights Act will change renting: Part 2, Fixed Terms
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Renters’ Rights Act 2025 is set to transform the UK rental market, with the abolition of fixed-term tenancies representing one of its most significant changes. Whilst the removal of Section 21 notices has dominated headlines, the shift to periodic tenancies by default could have equally far-reaching consequences for both tenants and landlords.

Understanding the shift from fixed-term tenancies

Under current rules, most tenancies begin with a fixed term – typically six or twelve months – during which neither party can normally end the agreement without consent. The new legislation will automatically convert all existing fixed-term tenancies into periodic (rolling) tenancies from 1st May 2026.

This change may surprise many renters who specifically sought longer fixed terms for stability, particularly:

  • Families wanting to secure their children's schooling
  • Professionals needing housing certainty during contract periods
  • Tenants who negotiated multi-year agreements with landlords

Potential benefits for tenants

The reforms aim to address several common rental market issues. Periodic tenancies could provide greater flexibility for tenants who find themselves in unsuitable properties or whose circumstances change unexpectedly. Notable scenarios include:

  • Properties with undisclosed issues like damp or mould becoming apparent after moving in
  • Job opportunities requiring relocation before a fixed term expires
  • Changes in personal circumstances making the property unsuitable

Previously, tenants in these situations often faced financial penalties or legal action if they attempted to leave fixed-term contracts early, even when facing genuine hardship.

Implications for landlords and the rental market

The changes present particular challenges for certain landlord segments. Student landlords, for instance, may find it harder to align tenancies with academic years. More broadly, the shift could:

  • Reduce landlords' ability to plan for void periods
  • Increase administrative work around tenancy management
  • Potentially affect rental pricing strategies

Whilst the reforms aim to create a fairer rental market, both tenants and landlords may wish to review their current agreements and prepare for the upcoming changes. Professional advice could help navigate this significant transition in housing policy.

item.title: Six ways the Renters Rights Act will change renting: Part 1, Section 21
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Renters’ Rights Act 2025 marks a significant shift in UK housing legislation, with its first stage coming into force on 1st May 2026. This reform promises to reshape the private rental sector, bringing changes comparable to those introduced by the Housing Act 1988 over three decades ago. Among its most talked-about provisions is the abolition of Section 21 ‘no-fault’ evictions, which could fundamentally alter the landlord-tenant dynamic.

Tenant empowerment under the new rules

The removal of Section 21 notices means tenants may feel more secure in challenging unfair practices. Currently, many renters hesitate to request repairs or dispute rent increases due to the threat of arbitrary eviction once their fixed term ends. The new legislation could change this dynamic by:

  • Allowing tenants to refuse unfavourable documentation like guarantees or new tenancy agreements
  • Providing formal channels to challenge rent increases through the First Tier Tribunal
  • Strengthening rights to demand essential repairs with compensation claims for non-compliance

Early reports suggest tenants are already preparing to exercise these new rights, potentially leading to longer tenancy periods. While landlords retain some grounds for possession – such as needing the property for personal use or sale – re-letting within 12 months could result in fines up to £40,000, creating a significant deterrent against misuse.

Impact on landlord-tenant relationships

The reforms may create tension as some landlords perceive a loss of control over their properties. This shift could lead to:

  • More thorough tenant vetting processes as landlords seek long-term reliability
  • Potential rent adjustments as landlords factor in reduced flexibility
  • Increased professionalisation of the sector with greater emphasis on proper maintenance

Whilst the changes aim to create fairer conditions for tenants, they also present challenges for landlords adapting to the new regulatory environment. Both parties may need to adjust their expectations and approaches to tenancy agreements.

As the first phase of these reforms takes effect, the property market will be watching closely to assess their real-world impact on rental dynamics, housing supply and landlord investment decisions. Future stages of the Act may introduce further changes to the UK’s rental landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The Renters’ Rights Act 2025 marks a significant shift in UK housing legislation, with its first stage coming into force on 1st May 2026. This reform promises to reshape the private rental sector, bringing changes comparable to those introduced by the Housing Act 1988 over three decades ago. Among its most talked-about provisions is the abolition of Section 21 ‘no-fault’ evictions, which could fundamentally alter the landlord-tenant dynamic.

Tenant empowerment under the new rules

The removal of Section 21 notices means tenants may feel more secure in challenging unfair practices. Currently, many renters hesitate to request repairs or dispute rent increases due to the threat of arbitrary eviction once their fixed term ends. The new legislation could change this dynamic by:

  • Allowing tenants to refuse unfavourable documentation like guarantees or new tenancy agreements
  • Providing formal channels to challenge rent increases through the First Tier Tribunal
  • Strengthening rights to demand essential repairs with compensation claims for non-compliance

Early reports suggest tenants are already preparing to exercise these new rights, potentially leading to longer tenancy periods. While landlords retain some grounds for possession – such as needing the property for personal use or sale – re-letting within 12 months could result in fines up to £40,000, creating a significant deterrent against misuse.

Impact on landlord-tenant relationships

The reforms may create tension as some landlords perceive a loss of control over their properties. This shift could lead to:

  • More thorough tenant vetting processes as landlords seek long-term reliability
  • Potential rent adjustments as landlords factor in reduced flexibility
  • Increased professionalisation of the sector with greater emphasis on proper maintenance

Whilst the changes aim to create fairer conditions for tenants, they also present challenges for landlords adapting to the new regulatory environment. Both parties may need to adjust their expectations and approaches to tenancy agreements.

As the first phase of these reforms takes effect, the property market will be watching closely to assess their real-world impact on rental dynamics, housing supply and landlord investment decisions. Future stages of the Act may introduce further changes to the UK’s rental landscape.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #433
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Recent research highlights growing tensions between landlords and letting agents, alongside concerns over rising possession cases and landlord preparedness for upcoming legislation. These findings shed light on key challenges facing the private rental sector (PRS) as it navigates evolving regulations and shifting dynamics.

Landlords Express Dissatisfaction with Letting Agents

A study by Goodlord reveals that whilst 68% of landlords rely on full management services from letting agents, 59% feel the fees charged do not reflect the value provided. Only 6% of landlords reported being satisfied with both the service and costs. The primary issue appears to be poor communication, with 48% citing this as their main frustration.

Letting agents, meanwhile, face their own challenges. Around 20% claim excessive administrative tasks limit their capacity to address other issues or expand their business. A Goodlord spokesperson noted that the problem lies not in effort or delivery but in the visibility of value. Improved communication between landlords and agents could help bridge this gap, particularly as new regulations reshape the PRS.

Sharp Increase in Possession Cases

Data from Landlord Action shows a significant rise in possession activity, with cases surging by 60% in March and enquiries increasing by 75%—the highest recorded by the organisation. In the first quarter of the year, Section 21 instructions rose by 43%. Concerns are mounting that the abolition of Section 21 could lead to a higher volume of Section 8 notices, placing additional strain on courts.

Landlord Action suggests that landlords who had previously delayed action are now progressing cases more swiftly. This trend highlights the urgency many feel as legislative changes loom, with the potential for increased complexity in managing tenant disputes.

Landlords Unprepared for Renters’ Reform Act

Alarming statistics from Togal indicate that 84% of landlords are not yet ready for the Renters’ Reform Act (RRA), with 58% at high risk of non-compliance. The free online self-assessment platform evaluated landlords across various criteria, including safety compliance, tenant communication documentation, tenancy agreements, repair tracking, and dispute resolution.

Out of nearly 400 landlords assessed, only 16% were deemed "mostly compliant," while the majority fell into the high-risk category. James Ashford of Togal emphasised the need for landlords to adopt a more meticulous approach, stating that simply claiming to have sent a document or completed a repair is no longer sufficient.

As the PRS adapts to new regulations and evolving expectations, landlords and letting agents alike may need to reassess their practices to ensure compliance and foster stronger working relationships. With significant changes on the horizon, proactive preparation could prove essential in navigating the challenges ahead.

item.title: Why Passive Fire Protection Is Becoming a Key Consideration for Property Investors
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Passive fire protection has evolved from a technical specification to a critical factor in property investment decisions. Where these systems were once viewed as mere regulatory box-ticking exercises, today’s investors are scrutinising fire safety measures with unprecedented rigour – particularly when assessing buy-to-let portfolios or commercial premises.

The Rising Importance of Passive Fire Protection

The tragic Grenfell Tower fire marked a turning point in how property professionals approach fire safety. Passive systems – including fire-resistant walls, floors, doors and cavity barriers – now feature prominently in due diligence checks. Investors might consider these hidden protections as important as visible safety features like alarms or extinguishers.

Several factors drive this shift:

  • Enhanced building safety regulations following the Building Safety Act 2022
  • Growing tenant awareness of fire safety standards
  • Mortgage lenders requesting detailed fire safety documentation
  • Insurance providers adjusting premiums based on protection levels

What Property Investors Need to Assess

For those considering property acquisitions, passive fire protection warrants careful evaluation. Key elements include:

  • Compartmentation: Fire-resistant barriers that contain blazes within specific areas
  • Structural protection: Measures safeguarding load-bearing elements from collapse
  • Penetration seals: Fire-stopping materials around pipes and cables
  • Documentation: Clear evidence of installation and maintenance records

Landlords and investors may wish to consult fire safety specialists when assessing properties, particularly for older buildings where original specifications might not meet current standards. Some mortgage providers now require fire risk assessments as part of their valuation process.

As fire safety continues moving up the property agenda, passive protection systems could become a significant factor in both investment decisions and long-term asset valuations. With regulations tightening and occupant safety paramount, these unseen safeguards may prove just as valuable as more visible property features.

item.title: High yields drive foreign and domestic investment
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Scotland’s property market is drawing significant attention from both domestic and international investors, with competitive rental yields proving particularly attractive. Industry experts highlight that regions north of the border continue to offer strong returns compared to other parts of the UK, creating opportunities for those looking to expand their portfolios.

Why Scotland appeals to property investors

Several factors contribute to Scotland’s growing popularity among buy-to-let investors. Unlike some southern English cities where property prices have reached record highs, many Scottish locations still offer relatively affordable entry points alongside healthy rental demand. Glasgow, Edinburgh and Aberdeen frequently feature in lists of top-performing rental markets, with yields often surpassing the UK average.

Key advantages for investors include:

  • Lower average property prices compared to London and the South East
  • Consistent tenant demand from students and young professionals
  • Strong rental growth in urban centres
  • Potential for capital appreciation in regenerating areas

International interest grows

While domestic investors continue to dominate the Scottish property market, overseas buyers are increasingly recognising its potential. The combination of sterling’s valuation and Scotland’s stable legal framework makes it an appealing option for international portfolios. Investors from Asia, the Middle East and Europe have shown particular interest in student accommodation and city-centre flats.

Property professionals note that foreign investors often favour turnkey developments or properties with guaranteed rental returns, especially in cities with large universities. The Scottish government’s relatively landlord-friendly policies, compared to some other UK regions, may also contribute to this growing international appeal.

For those considering Scottish property investment, thorough research into local market conditions remains essential. While yields appear attractive, factors such as property management costs, potential void periods and upcoming regulatory changes should all form part of the decision-making process.

item.title: Landlords’ share of Stamp Duty on the rise
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Stamp Duty receipts increasingly driven by landlords and second homes

Recent analysis of government data by Paragon Bank reveals a significant shift in Stamp Duty contributions across England. In over half of local authorities, transactions involving second homes and buy-to-let properties now account for the majority of Stamp Duty receipts.

What the figures reveal

The data highlights how private landlords and second homeowners are becoming increasingly important to the Treasury's property tax revenues. This trend may reflect several factors in the current housing market:

  • The continued demand for rental properties across many regions
  • Higher Stamp Duty rates applied to additional properties
  • Potential shifts in housing tenure patterns post-pandemic

Implications for the property market

For those considering property investment, these findings might influence decisions around timing and location. Areas where landlords contribute significantly to Stamp Duty receipts could indicate:

  • Strong rental demand in those locations
  • Potential competition from other investors
  • Local authorities that may be more sensitive to policy changes affecting landlords

Prospective buyers might wish to research local market conditions thoroughly before committing to purchases, particularly where Stamp Duty liabilities could be higher for investment properties. As always, consulting with a qualified mortgage adviser could help navigate these considerations.
